correctness.rationale: Core alignment, lag handling, FDR, and leakage guards are largely correct, but AIPW uses random cross-fitting on a time series, E-value CI uses the wrong bound, and backtest prediction intervals are miscalibrated and mismatched to the documented nominal.

robustness.rationale: Good NaN handling, cell-size gates, embargoed walk-forward, and mediator exclusions; a few fragile spots remain (bfill of confounders; PI calibration).

scalability.rationale: All methods are linear or near-linear in N with small per-run constants; batching to Postgres and incremental backtesting will comfortably handle 10× data.

idiomaticness.rationale: Uses standard libraries (scikit-learn, statsmodels, SHAP) in mostly idiomatic ways; one notable exception is time-series AIPW using shuffled KFold.

response.summary: Overall, this is a thoughtful single-subject pipeline with careful behavioral-day alignment, correct target shifting, strong leakage guards (28‑day embargo, lagged/rolled features computed with shift(1)), and consistent multiple-comparison control for all high-surface families. The SARIMAX exog shift and the XGBoost feature pool are defensible; Stage‑3 OLS caps predictors and uses HAC errors appropriately. The most material weaknesses are in the causal layer: AIPW cross-fitting uses shuffled KFold on an autocorrelated outcome, which risks optimistic estimates/SEs; confounders are imputed with bfill (future information); and the E‑value CI uses the wrong bound for negative effects. On the predictive side, daily and backtest XGBoost prediction intervals are calibrated at ±1.645σ (≈90%) while the schema/labels say 80%; in the multi-horizon backtest σ is taken from in-sample training residuals, which will under-cover. These are fixable with modest code changes. Riskiest assumptions: that random cross-fitting breaks no time dependence; that backfilled confounders are innocuous; and that PI coverage is close to nominal. If I could only fix three things: (1) switch AIPW cross-fitting to a time-respecting split (TimeSeriesSplit or blocked folds) and remove any future-informed scaling; (2) compute PIs consistently at the documented nominal (80%) and use out-of-fold residuals for backtest intervals; (3) correct the E‑value CI computation to use the confidence limit nearest the null (upper bound for negative ATEs) and use the pooled within-group SD for d.

findings.title: AIPW cross-fitting uses shuffled KFold on autocorrelated HRV
findings.description: estimate_aipw applies KFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True) to a daily time series with known HRV autocorrelation, allowing adjacent days to land in different folds and letting models indirectly see near-future outcomes.
findings.evidence: In estimate_aipw: `kf = KFold(n_splits=n_folds, shuffle=True, random_state=42)` then folds are used to fit ps_model and outcome models; no time-order constraint is enforced.
findings.recommendation: Use TimeSeriesSplit (or blocked, non-shuffled folds) for cross-fitting, and include hrv_lag1 explicitly if not already; alternatively perform rolling-origin cross-fitting so training strictly precedes validation in time.

findings.title: E-value CI uses wrong bound for negative effects
findings.description: compute_e_value takes only ci_low and always uses it to form the CI-based E-value; for negative ATEs the bound nearest the null is typically the upper bound, so using the lower bound inflates robustness.
findings.evidence: Function signature `compute_e_value(ate, ci_low, pooled_sd)`; logic sets `d_ci = ci_low/pooled_sd` for ate>0, else uses `-ci_low` if ci_low<0; ci_high is never passed or considered.
findings.recommendation: Pass both ci_low and ci_high; compute the bound nearest zero (lower for positive ATE, upper for negative ATE), then apply the Chinn transform and VanderWeele–Ding formula accordingly.

findings.title: Confounder imputation bfills future values in causal prep
findings.description: In _prepare_treatment, confounders are imputed with `X_df.ffill().bfill()`, which can use future observations to fill missing pre-treatment covariates, violating the temporal ordering required for identification.
findings.evidence: Code: `X_df = sub[list(confounders)].copy(); X_df = X_df.ffill().bfill()`; no restriction to forward-only imputation.
findings.recommendation: Restrict imputation to forward-fill only (ffill) and drop remaining missing rows, or use model-based imputation trained on past data only; document any remaining missingness-driven drops.

findings.title: Prediction intervals miscalibrated and nominal mismatch (90% vs 80%)
findings.description: XGBoost PIs are constructed as ±1.645σ (≈90%) but the schema comments and historical dashboards describe 80%; in the multi-horizon backtest σ is taken from training residuals, which yields optimistic coverage.
findings.evidence: train_xgboost: `test_lower = test_pred - 1.645 * pred_std` (and same for upper). In run_evaluation XGBoost backtest uses `residuals_std = np.std(y_tr - m.predict(X_tr))` per fold.
findings.recommendation: Decide on a single nominal (80% or 90%) and use it consistently across training, backtest, and storage; for backtest, compute σ from out-of-fold or validation residuals within each window rather than in-sample training residuals.

findings.title: AIPW standardization fits on full sample before cross-fitting
findings.description: Features are standardized via _standardize(X) before fold splits, so test-fold scaling parameters are influenced by the held-out data, which is non-ideal for cross-fitting and time series.
findings.recommendation: Move scaling inside the fold loop: fit the scaler on Xt (train fold) and transform both Xt and Xv per fold; for time series, use parameters from past-only data.

findings.title: Explicit binary 'negative_split' can be non-binary and gets silently dropped
findings.description: Daily negative_split is computed as the mean of per-activity flags, which can produce 0.5 on multi-activity days; enumeration later requires values ⊆ {0,1}, causing the intended binary treatment to be skipped.
findings.recommendation: Convert daily negative_split to a strict 0/1 (e.g., any activity with negative split ⇒ 1) before enumeration, or round the mean to {0,1}.

findings.title: E-value effect size uses overall SD, not pooled within-group SD
findings.description: Cohen’s d for continuous outcomes should use the pooled within-group SD; using the overall SD can misstate the magnitude and thus the E-value.
findings.evidence: _estimate_one sets `pooled_outcome_sd = Y.std(ddof=1)`; compute_e_value then divides ate by this.
findings.recommendation: Compute pooled SD: sqrt(((n1−1)s1² + (n0−1)s0²)/(n1+n0−2)), and pass that value into compute_e_value.

findings.title: Backtest PIs recorded without coverage for baselines; horizon label may mislead CI chart
findings.description: Only XGBoost backtest rows carry PI bands; baselines lack lower/upper, but ci_coverage is aggregated per model×horizon without guardrails, which can confuse downstream coverage displays.
findings.evidence: In run_evaluation, baselines append rows without prediction_lower/upper; compute_metrics handles None, but stored metrics may mix models/horizons with and without CIs.
findings.recommendation: Either compute comparable PIs for baselines (e.g., empirical residual σ) or explicitly label/omit CI coverage for models without PIs to avoid misleading comparisons.

things_done_well.title: Time-respecting backtest with 28‑day embargo to prevent rolling-window leakage
things_done_well.why_it_matters: Ensures no overlap between training windows and evaluation targets when 28‑day rolling HRV baselines are used, preventing subtle target leakage in a single-user time series.

things_done_well.title: Consistent BH-FDR across high-surface families and exclusion of autocorrelation artifacts
things_done_well.why_it_matters: Applying FDR to Spearman, journal, habit, supplement, and nutrition protects against spurious findings in n=1 exploratory analyses; excluding HRV transforms avoids trivial persistence dominating drivers.

things_done_well.title: Causal DAG respects mediator exclusion and uses pre-treatment lags only
things_done_well.why_it_matters: Avoids conditioning on same-night sleep/recovery mediators, so estimated effects remain actionable total effects (what changes if I intervene tonight?).

things_done_well.title: SARIMAX exogenous features correctly shifted and residual diagnostics logged
things_done_well.why_it_matters: Shifting exog by one day keeps causal alignment intact; Ljung–Box and ADF checks add needed feedback on model adequacy and stationarity.

things_done_well.title: Out-of-fold residual σ for XGBoost PIs (training stage) with TimeSeriesSplit
things_done_well.why_it_matters: Calibrating σ from time-ordered out-of-fold residuals produces more realistic predictive uncertainty than a single validation slice.

questions_for_followup: The schema comments say '80% PI' in pds.hrv_predictions, but code uses ±1.645σ (~90%). Which nominal should the UI and metrics assume, and should we migrate stored bands to match?
Do dashboards currently filter journal/habit/supplement result tables by passes_fdr, or do they display raw p-values/q-values? A screenshot or query would clarify presentation intent.
For AIPW, can we confirm empirical HRV autocorrelation (e.g., ρ1) on this dataset to choose fold sizes/block lengths for time-respecting cross-fitting?
Should 'negative_split' be treated as 'any negative split that day' or 'all activities negative split'? Clarifying the intended semantics will inform binarization.
Is baseline CI coverage (hrv_model_metrics.ci_coverage) plotted alongside XGBoost/SARIMAX/Prophet? If so, do we want to compute comparable bands for baselines or hide coverage for those models?
